original_abstract:
Developing general-purpose machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) that remain accurate across diverse reactive chemical environments is a major challenge in computational chemistry. While traditional physics-based models often force a trade-off between computational efficiency and broad applicability, machine learning offers a path toward quantum-level accuracy at a fraction of the cost. In this work, we present ANI-1xnr, a general reactive MLIP for systems containing carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen (CHNO).

ANI-1xnr was developed using an automated active learning workflow driven by "nanoreactor" simulations. This approach uses an ensemble of neural networks to iteratively sample condensed-phase reactive space, discovering over 1,000 unique molecular species and their corresponding reaction pathways without prior human bias or pre-defined reaction networks. To validate its generality, we applied the potential to five complex case studies: carbon solid-phase nucleation, graphene ring formation from acetylene, biofuel additive ignition, methane combustion, and the prebiotic synthesis of glycine.

In each application, ANI-1xnr produced results consistent with experimental data and traditional ab initio or reactive force field methods, despite not being specifically trained on these systems. The model effectively captures structural phase transitions, substituent effects on ignition, and complex molecular synthesis in extreme environments. By enabling high-throughput, chemically accurate simulations of condensed-phase reactions, ANI-1xnr serves as a robust tool for exploring new frontiers in chemistry and materials science.
humanized_abstract:
Developing general-purpose machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) that are applicable across a wide range of reactive chemical environments is one of the open problems in computational chemistry. Physics-based potentials offer accuracy at the quantum level but generally do not provide the computational speed and flexibility needed for general purpose applications. Machine learning allows for a different optimization of the trade space between model accuracy, computational speed, and flexibility. Here we introduce ANI-1xnr, a general reactive MLIP for the chemically diverse CHNO element set.

ANI-1xnr was constructed using an automated active learning workflow initiated by “nanoreactor” simulations. The resultingANI captures a diverse ensemble of conditioned condensed-phase reactive space using an ensemble of neural networks and thereby identified approximately 1,000 distinct small molecules and associated reaction pathways in the liquid phase, all in the absence of a priori knowledge (bias) and predefined reaction networks. The transferability of the potential to a range of complex systems that embody diverse mechanisms has been demonstrated through five case studies including carbon solid phase nucleation, graphene ring formation from acetylene, biofuel additive ignition, methane combustion and the prebiotic synthesis of glycine.

Each of the applications shown demonstrates that ANI-1xnr behavior is consistent with experimental measurements and traditional ab initio or reactive force field simulations, yet has not been trained to the specific systems. The structural phase transitions, the effects of different substituents on ignition, and the complex molecular synthesis in extreme conditions are all examples of the high-throughput, chemically accurate simulation of condensed-phase chemical reactions that ANI-1xnr makes possible.

Developing general-purpose machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) that are applicable across a wide range of reactive chemical environments is one of the open problems in computational chemistry. Machine learning allows for a different optimization of the trade space between model accuracy, computational speed, and flexibility. ANI-1xnr was constructed using an automated active learning workflow initiated by “nanoreactor” simulations. The resultingANI captures a diverse ensemble of conditioned condensed-phase reactive space using an ensemble of neural networks and thereby identified approximately 1,000 distinct small molecules and associated reaction pathways in the liquid phase, all in the absence of a priori knowledge (bias) and predefined reaction networks. The transferability of the potential to a range of complex systems that embody diverse mechanisms has been demonstrated through five case studies including carbon solid phase nucleation, graphene ring formation from acetylene, biofuel additive ignition, methane combustion and the prebiotic synthesis of glycine.

Each of the applications shown demonstrates that ANI-1xnr behavior is consistent with experimental measurements and traditional ab initio or reactive force field simulations, yet has not been trained to the specific systems. document.input:
Developing general-purpose machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) that remain accurate across diverse reactive chemical environments is a major challenge in computational chemistry. While traditional physics-based models often force a trade-off between computational efficiency and broad applicability, machine learning offers a path toward quantum-level accuracy at a fraction of the cost. In each application, ANI-1xnr produced results consistent with experimental data and traditional ab initio or reactive force field methods, despite not being specifically trained on these systems.
